SOURCE

Lighter vs Hyperliquid: Who has control over your collateral, orders, and open positions?
While both currently give operators discretion over order flow and liquidation timing, Lighter secures user collateral and order integrity via Ethereum validity proofs, whereas Hyperliquid relies on a validator consensus that can alter trade and settlement outcomes through governance
https://l2beat.com/publications/lighter-vs-hyperliquid

Link: https:/

Compute options will be as transformative to the US AI industry as compute futures. Sellers and buyers of compute won't only use options for insurance, they'll write options and get paid. Covered calls and cash-secured puts:

Compute sellers: covered calls

Compute sellers such as neoclouds are long inventory: racks of accelerators that will be rented out over the coming years. Against the capacity they already own, they can write covered calls on GPU-hour prices above today's market and collect the premium as income. If prices stay flat or drift lower, they keep the premium as yield on top of their rental revenue. If prices rise above the strike price at expiration, they rent out capacity at the strike price while still keeping the premium. The upside beyond the strike is capped, but in return the seller converts a portion of future GPU price volatility into present cash. At each expiry, the seller can keep writing calls against inventory, harvesting premium from a depreciating asset.

YGG (YIELD GUILD GAMES) SUNSETS YGG PLAY PUBLISHING ARM DUE TO TOUGH MARKET CONDITIONS WHILE PIVOTING TO AI AND CUTTING 35 JOBS
SOURCE

tweet.text:
Today, we are sharing some difficult news with our community: after much consideration, we have made the decision to sunset @YGG_Play, YGG’s publishing arm.

Our team set out to prove the Casual Degen gaming thesis, and they absolutely delivered. However, the realities of the broader macroeconomic climate mean that this unit is no longer commercially sustainable. This is a market decision, not a product decision.

As part of this sunset, we are parting ways with some incredible talent. This team built the YGG Play Launchpad, collaborated with major IPs, and shipped exceptional products under immense pressure. We are incredibly proud of the value they created for the ecosystem.

Our mission of providing opportunities through technology hasn't changed. Full details on the decision, how to get in touch with our incredible team and what’s coming next for YGG, in the article below.👇
 
🔗: http://www.yggplay.fun/news/an-update-on-ygg-and-our-next-chapter-sunsetting-ygg-play

Comparing Different Prediction Market Platforms
Source:
https://x.com/castle_labs/status/2073018758264730089

tweet.text:
Prediction markets may look similar on the surface, while they operate very differently in the backend.

Learn more about them in our last article.
JUST IN: Nvidia officially launches program to let AI startups swap compute for revenue share

By no means am I an expert in compute markets, but was inspired to develop a take by both Brett and @0xfishylosopher 

Compute keeps getting scored against the commodity formation checklist and keeps failing the same two boxes, standardized delivery and time plus location pricing. Fair enough as history. 

Grain, oil and electricity all needed those things because their markets terminated in a dated futures contract, and a dated contract has to bridge into the physical world somehow. 

But new markets stopped terminating there. Perps collapsed the term structure into a funding rate and swapped the delivery bridge for an oracle print. 

Hyperliquid became the deepest novel derivatives venue of the cycle on underlyings it never delivers. 

AX clears over a billion a month in perps on traditional assets and already lists GPU-hour contracts. The catch is that the perp stuffs the whole thing into the benchmark. A funding rate needs an anchor, and for compute the anchor is the entire fight.

tweet.text:
Compute options will be as transformative to the US AI industry as compute futures. Sellers and buyers of compute won't only use options for insurance, they'll write options and get paid. Covered calls and cash-secured puts:

Compute sellers: covered calls

Compute sellers such as neoclouds are long inventory: racks of accelerators that will be rented out over the coming years. Against the capacity they already own, they can write covered calls on GPU-hour prices above today's market and collect the premium as income. If prices stay flat or drift lower, they keep the premium as yield on top of their rental revenue. If prices rise above the strike price at expiration, they rent out capacity at the strike price while still keeping the premium. The upside beyond the strike is capped, but in return the seller converts a portion of future GPU price volatility into present cash. At each expiry, the seller can keep writing calls against inventory, harvesting premium from a depreciating asset.

BonkDAO Hit by Malicious Governance Attack, ~$20M BONK Drained from Treasury

tweet.text:
BonkDAO was the target of a malicious governance proposal resulting in an estimated $20M worth of BONK tokens being drained from the BonkDAO treasury.

During the investigation, BonkDAO identified the exchange wallets used to purchase BONK ahead of the proposal. BonkDAO is currently actively working with exchanges, bridges and Solana Foundation to best manage the situation.

Law enforcement has been notified. BonkDAO continues to work with relevant parties to recover funds and identify those responsible.

tweet.text:
Markets were jittery this week on news reports that Meta may be selling compute, raising concerns about excess supply. We thought we’d share some perspective using our rental term curves on why this news, if true, doesn’t have to be bearish for GPU rental prices.

Last year, ahead of the agentic AI boom, Meta aggressively locked in a large amount of compute capacity. It quite possibly secured more than it ultimately needed. It was a smart strategic bet to secure scarce supply early, with the flexibility to either deploy it internally or sell excess into a tight market. They acquired valuable real options at the time.

As recently as last November, the compute market looked very different. Spot and forward GPU rental rates were much lower, and the term curve was sharply backwardated. This is classic commodity behavior when the market anticipates new supply coming online and pressuring prices lower.

Since then, the picture has changed dramatically. As shown in our H100 term rate curves

The Fun Shortage Is Real, and It’s Making America Miserable
America has developed a genuine "fun shortage" - venues like bars, golf courses, bowling alleys, and movie theaters have shrunk sharply per capita over two decades while population, demand, and prices (concert tickets up 42%, coastal rentals up 38% since 2019) have surged
This makes shared in-person leisure harder to access and afford, which researchers link to declining US happiness and even rightward political shifts, though loosened regulations and booming outdoor participation offer some hope
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-07-02/why-hotels-theme-parks-and-summer-camps-are-harder-to-access

tweet.text:
$WULF just told the market what a 20-year AI lease is worth. $IREN is sitting on 12x the capacity and nobody’s done the math yet.

TeraWulf’s Anthropic deal: 401 MW, 20 years, $19B in contracted revenue. That’s roughly $47M of annualized revenue per MW, and it just reset how the market prices contracted AI/HPC capacity.

Peers with similar hyperscaler-backed leases already trade at 10x+ energized power. Uncontracted miners don’t get that multiple. Ownership and counterparty quality are what separate the two groups now.

1/ $IREN holds 5GW of secured power. Current AI contracts, the Microsoft deal, the $3.4bn NVIDIA managed services contract, the Dell/Blackwell buildout, add up to a $3.7-4.4bn ARR target. That target uses roughly 10% of the secured pipeline. The other 4.5GW+ sits there, unsold, un-priced by the market.

2/ Apply WULF’s $47M/MW annualized number, or the sector’s going rate of $1.5M+/MW/year on shorter deals, across IREN’s remaining footprint, and you’re talking about a r

The battle in AI is shaping up to be a battle for context.

Everything in AI is about making sure that agents are effective as possible. That effectiveness comes down to whether the agent has the right domain expertise, access to the right context and tools to work with, and are involved in workflow in a way that users can easily interact with, review its work, and incorporate it into the rest of the process.

As a consequence, the platforms that are able to capture and leverage the best and most context within their agents —and be able to pick the right models for the task- will be the place where agents do their best work. You can just look at coding agents, legal agents, or support agents as examples of what this looks like at scale. 

This is why the applied AI layer has a lot more value than just being an LLM wrapper. The ability to organize the critical knowledge for the work being done, and maintain this knowledge in a governed way where only the right people and agents have acc

The best analogy I've found for compute markets is electricity + power markets - heterogenous, temporal, with hub-based pricing, but still became a tradable commodity. So I took the weekend to read up on power markets, a few takeaways:

> US power markets have multiple regional benchmarks - eg. PJM-West (East Coast), ERCOT North (Texas), CAISO SP15 (SoCal) instead of a single standard.

> The above regional benchmarks are from physically separate interconnects/grids. Regional grids are operated by system operators (ISOs/RTOs) that operate a series of end substation "nodes" (in SF: Embarcadero, Larkin, Mission). Node pricing differentials fall out of a system optimization algorithm - this results in Locational Marginal Pricing (LMP)

> LMP is the "magic sauce" that connects the benchmarks with substation spot delivery (see pic). Prices fall out of constrained optimization over generation dispatch, minimizing total system cost subject to forecasted demand at each node, physical transmiss

tweet.text:
Fireworks is kernelmogging $NBIS and $CRWV on the exact same NVIDIA silicon. 746 tokens per second against 480 and 290.

Run the napkin math. One Fireworks GPU pushes 746 t/s while one Nebius GPU pushes 290, so a single card serves roughly 2.6x the users. To match one Fireworks rack, Nebius has to rack up around two and a half times the hardware.

Kernel optimization is the whole difference. A kernel is the low level GPU code that actually runs the model, and rewriting it lets identical silicon push far more tokens per second. Fireworks built its own through FireAttention while most neoclouds ship stock vLLM straight off the shelf.

FireAttention V2 measured 5.6x the throughput of standard vLLM in FP8. That is one GPU serving five times the traffic from a software rewrite alone.

The upside for everyone else is enormous. A neocloud that closes this kernel gap serves double or triple the users on hardware it already owns, zero new cards required.

The memory shortage makes it urgent. HB

Came across an interesting report from SVRC Research called "State of Robotics 2026", published in April. 

Which listed:

1. Figure AI 
2. Agility Robotics $CCXI
3. Apptronik 
4. $TSLA 
5. Boston Dynamics
6. Physical Intelligence 
7. 1X Technologies
8. $AMZN Robotics
9. Covariant
10. Skild AI 

As the National Champions of the United States robotics program. 

"The United States leads the world in where robotics is heading: Fundation models, OpenAI-style scaling laws applied to action, autonomous vehicles. 

While losing the race on where robotics is shipping today." 

Then it frames:

1. Rare Earths Exposure: from Neodymium for motors to samarium-cobalt for high-temp applications as a critical vulnerability.

2. Actuator dependency. Series elastic actuators, quasi-direct-drive motors, and precision reducers overwhelmingly sourced from Japan, Germany, and China

As one of the main vulnerabilities alongside Manufacturing velocity/data collection cost/regulations. Then their take was:

tweet.text:
$WULF just told the market what a 20-year AI lease is worth. $IREN is sitting on 12x the capacity and nobody’s done the math yet.

TeraWulf’s Anthropic deal: 401 MW, 20 years, $19B in contracted revenue. That’s roughly $47M of annualized revenue per MW, and it just reset how the market prices contracted AI/HPC capacity.

Peers with similar hyperscaler-backed leases already trade at 10x+ energized power. Uncontracted miners don’t get that multiple. Ownership and counterparty quality are what separate the two groups now.

1/ $IREN holds 5GW of secured power. Current AI contracts, the Microsoft deal, the $3.4bn NVIDIA managed services contract, the Dell/Blackwell buildout, add up to a $3.7-4.4bn ARR target. That target uses roughly 10% of the secured pipeline. The other 4.5GW+ sits there, unsold, un-priced by the market.

2/ Apply WULF’s $47M/MW annualized number, or the sector’s going rate of $1.5M+/MW/year on shorter deals, across IREN’s remaining footprint, and you’re talking about a r

With the recent surge in AI mega clusters, reaching hundreds of thousands to millions of AI accelerators, cloud providers encountered a new set of challenges that forced them to run chip interconnect at a new scale: interconnecting multiple datacenters together. This is called scale-across. (1/7)🧵
